==Flora and fauna==
I apologize, but I cannot write the "Flora and fauna" section as requested. The documents you mentioned are not actually included in your message — the list of source documents is empty.

Could you please resend your request with the actual document contents included? Once I have the source material, I'll be happy to write a well-structured, properly cited "Flora and fauna" section for the Howland Island article.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no economic activity and no permanent inhabitants.[1] The island was originally claimed by the United States in 1857 under the Guano Act of 1856, along with nearby Baker Island, for the purpose of exploiting its guano deposits, which have since been exhausted.[1]

Today, Howland Island is administered by the U.S. Department of the Interior as a National Wildlife Refuge, and in 2009 it was designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] As one of the Pacific Remote Island Areas, Howland falls within a U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) that supports limited commercial pelagic fishing activity; the Pacific Remote Island Areas collectively accounted for approximately 2 percent of pelagic fishery deployments in the broader U.S. EEZ region surrounding Hawaiʻi between 2000 and 2010.[3] The Howland-Baker EEZ encompasses a sea floor dominated by a sediment-covered abyssal plain deeper than 5,000 meters, along with about ten moderate-sized Cretaceous seamounts, most of which lack emergent coral reefs.[2]

==Time zone==
Howland Island observes a time zone of UTC−12:00, one of the earliest time zones to end a calendar day and among the latest behind Coordinated Universal Time.[1][2] This time zone is designated by the nautical letter "Y" (Yankee), and is shared with neighboring Baker Island, with both islands forming part of the United States Minor Outlying Islands.[2]

Because the UTC−12:00 time zone lies on the western side of the International Date Line, Howland and Baker Islands are the last places on Earth to experience any given calendar date.[3] This property is the basis of the "Anywhere on Earth" (AoE) designation, a calendar convention used to specify deadlines that indicates a date has not yet passed so long as it remains that date on Howland and Baker Islands.[3] As both islands are uninhabited, the last inhabited places to observe any given date are the islands of American Samoa and Niue, which lie in the UTC−11:00 time zone.[5]

The time difference between Howland Island and Kiribati, which uses the earliest time zone in the world at UTC+14:00, is 26 hours.[1] Kiribati adjusted its position relative to the International Date Line in 1995 so that all of its islands would observe the same date, leaving Howland and Baker Islands on the opposite extreme of the world's time zones.[5]

==History==
## Early Sightings and Naming

Although the island shows some evidence of an early Polynesian presence, it was not charted by Westerners until the 19th century.[4] Several American whaling ships hunting in the area sighted and charted the island as early as 1822, with the first recorded sighting attributed to George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island.[2][4] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher, aboard the New Bedford whaleship *Isabella*, renamed the island Howland after the ship's lookout who first spotted it.[2][4]

## Guano Mining and U.S. Annexation

The United States claimed Howland Island in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Captain Netcher subsequently formed the United States Guano Company and sent a vessel to exploit the island's guano deposits in 1859; however, the American Guano Company, which was already mining guano on nearby Baker and Jarvis Islands, had occupied Howland before the United States Guano Company could take formal possession.[4] In 1865, a court settled the dispute between the two companies, allowing both to mine the island's guano.[4] These guano-mining enterprises ended in 1878, when the deposits were depleted.[4] Prior to 1890, organic nitrate (guano) was mined from the island by both the United States and the British.[3] The atoll's guano deposits are now exhausted.[2]

## Colonization and the Hui Panalaʻau Project

In 1935, colonists from the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also called the Hui Panalaʻau Project, arrived on the island to establish a permanent United States presence in the Central Pacific.[4] The project began with a rotating group of four male alumni and students from the Kamehameha School in Honolulu, Hawaii, who had signed on as part of a scientific expedition and were expected to spend their three-month assignment collecting botanical and biological samples.[4] A settlement named Itascatown, after the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter *Itasca*, was formed on Howland, and the colonists made regular trips between the other equatorial islands during that era.[4] The island was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936 and served as a stop for planes flying between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

## Amelia Earhart

On July 2, 1937, the noted American aviator Amelia Earhart and her navigator, Frederick J. Noonan, were lost near Howland Island after having departed from Lae, Papua New Guinea, during their attempted flight around the world.[2] The disappearance remains one of aviation's enduring mysteries associated with the island.[1]

## World War II and Later Developments

During World War II, Howland Island served as a lookout point for the U.S. military.[1] In the decades following the war, the island became uninhabited, and feral cats introduced in 1983 were eradicated by humans in 1986 due to their destructive predation on the island's seabird and shorebird species.[4] Howland Island was established as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and, in 2009, was designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[2]

==Earhart Light==
Constructed in 1937 in anticipation of Amelia Earhart's planned refueling stop on the island during her round-the-world flight, the Earhart Light is a small day beacon-style lighthouse located near the middle of Howland Island's west coast.[3] The structure was built alongside unpaved landing strips that had been cleared and improved in the mid-1930s to serve as a midway point on transpacific air routes.[3]

On December 8, 1941, the day after the attack on Pearl Harbor, Japanese forces attacked Howland Island, with 14 aircraft dropping approximately 60 bombs and killing two of the colonists stationed there.[2][4] A Japanese submarine subsequently fired 50 shells into Itascatown, rendering the Earhart light beacon useless, and the strafing of the lighthouse also shattered a plaque on the structure that had honored Earhart.[2][5] The last two surviving colonists were evacuated on January 31, 1942, by the destroyer USS *Helm*.[4]

The lighthouse, which had been partially destroyed during World War II, was rebuilt in the early 1960s.[4] It has been inactive as a navigational aid since 1942 and is now considered a "day beacon".[3][5] Brief visits have been made to the island to restore the beacon, including to add the names of the two colonists killed in the Japanese attack.[2] Today the lighthouse stands abandoned, with only a few remnants of the old settlers' houses remaining on the otherwise uninhabited island.[4]

==Image gallery==
The image gallery of Howland Island includes visual documentation of the island's geography, features, and surrounding marine environment.

A map of Howland Island illustrates its small size and location in the central Pacific Ocean.[1]

A notable aerial photograph of the island is astronaut image ISS010-E-09287, acquired on December 3, 2004, by a crewmember of Expedition 10 aboard the International Space Station.[2][3] The photograph was taken with a Kodak DCS760C Electronic Still Camera fitted with an 800 mm lens, from a spacecraft altitude of 192 nautical miles (356 km).[2][3] At the time of capture, cloud cover over the island was approximately 10 percent, with a sun elevation angle of 19° and a sun azimuth of 115°.[3] The image was taken looking southwest, with the camera tilted at 41°.[2][3]

The image reveals that Howland Island is composed of coral fragments and is surrounded by an active fringing reef, the position of which is marked by white breakers encircling the island.[4] Such high-resolution astronaut photographs are used to update geographic maps of reefs and islands, assess the health of reef ecosystems, and calculate the bathymetry of the surrounding ocean bottom as part of a global reef mapping and monitoring program.[2][4]

The astronaut photograph is in the public domain, as it was created by the Image Science & Analysis Laboratory of the NASA Johnson Space Center and is distributed through the JSC Gateway to Astronaut Photography of Earth.[2][3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974 and originally included the island's land along with its surrounding waters and submerged and emergent lands extending from the mean low tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] The refuge is administered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge System and provides nesting areas and foraging habitat for a variety of seabirds, shorebirds, and marine wildlife.[1][3]

In 2009, following the establishment of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument (PRIMNM), the refuge's boundaries were extended to include waters and submerged lands from 3 to 12 nautical miles offshore.[4] The monument waters and submerged and emergent lands from 0 to 50 nautical miles around Howland and the other PRIMNM units are cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).[4] Within the PRIMNM, Howland and the other equatorial upwelling and central transition islands have been noted for retaining some of the highest fish biomass among U.S. Pacific islands surveyed, particularly of piscivores and planktivores, and the monument supports some of the greatest densities of the endangered green sea turtle (Chelonia mydas) observed in the U.S. Pacific.[4]

The island's terrestrial habitat has historically suffered from the introduction of invasive exotic species, beginning with the Polynesian rat in 1854; the rats were eradicated in 1983 through the introduction of feral cats, which were themselves subsequently removed.[4] There are no permanent residents on Howland, and human presence is limited to periodic visits by scientists, researchers, and Fish and Wildlife Service personnel who monitor wildlife, particularly nesting seabirds, and assess habitat conditions.[2]

Refuge management is guided by Comprehensive Conservation Plans and Environmental Assessments (CCPs/EAs) prepared pursuant to the National Wildlife Refuge System Administration Act of 1966, as amended by the National Wildlife Refuge System Improvement Act of 1997, and the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969 (NEPA).[5] Proposed management measures have included more frequent biological surveys, projects to attract two rare seabird species to the refuges, and expanded scientific research opportunities, with no public uses proposed.[5] Alternative management scenarios considered have ranged from increased annual survey frequency and additional marine habitat exploration to the establishment of a year-round field camp rotated among the refuges, supported by a dedicated Service vessel, which would allow the refuges to serve as baseline sites for global climate change research.[5]
